unitTitle: Y8 Developing for the web (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Website building blocks — HTML tags & inline styling
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what HTML is
I can use HTML tags to structure a web page
I can modify a tag with inline styling to change how it looks
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: HTML · tag · formatting. TA: everyone has a plain text editor + the starter HTML file open. Read the three “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–5) Starter — “work smarter, not harder” (think–pair–share, the brick-wall guesstimate on the starter worksheet). Pupils CHOOSE/estimate, no long writing. • Support: tick-box “computers are good at jobs done again and again”. • Core: explain why we automate. • Challenge: name a repeated computer job to automate. 2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — what HTML is. A web page is plain text; tags mark it up (`

` opens, `

` closes). Match the tag to its job on the worksheet (drag). • Support: card-recognition (brackets, the closing /). • Core: predict what `

` renders. • Challenge: explain `color:red;`. Likely error: missing closing tag → fix-words “every tag needs a partner that closes with a /.” 3. (20–40) You-do — build a page (Parsons: order the HTML lines), then add an inline style (`style="color:red;text-align:center;"`). Keep a screenshot. • Support: “change THIS tag” arrow card. • Core: edit unaided. • Challenge: style the whole ``. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” 4. (40–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the three “I can…”; show their styled page. TA notes who closed every tag and used a style modifier. Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Words are not enough — images & building from a design
lessons.objectives:
I can add an image to a web page with the img tag
I can name what src and alt do
I can build a web page to match a given design
lessons.outline: Routine first. Vocabulary on board: image · tag · attribute · directory · render. TA: starter recap file + the target design image ready; pupils have an image file in their page folder. Read the three “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–10) Starter — tags recap (fill in the missing closing tags on the starter worksheet, with the tag-reference image). Note ``/`` also help screen readers. • Support: tick “a closing tag starts with ` over ``. 2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — the img tag. ``: `src` = the file name, `alt` = a description for screen readers / if the image fails. Match the part to its job (drag). Predict the render. • Support: card-sort tags vs attributes. • Core: why `src` needs the file extension. • Challenge: write a full img tag. Likely error: image does not appear → fix-words “the file name and extension must match exactly.” 3. (25–45) You-do — build a page to match the target design (order the steps to add an image). Keep a screenshot. • Support: printed copy of the design + step strip. • Core: build it on-screen. • Challenge: add a second image + alt text. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” 4. (45–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; show the page with its image. TA notes who set both `src` and `alt`. Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Taking shortcuts — styling with CSS
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what CSS is
I can use CSS to style a web page
I can explain why CSS is better than styling by hand
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: CSS · style · head · body · attribute. TA: a starter HTML + CSS file pair per pupil; remote view to share work. Read the three “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–5) Starter — the slow way (think–pair–share: styling 20 paragraphs by hand). Draw out: slow and inconsistent; “work smarter, not harder”. • Support: tick “slow and easy to get wrong”. • Core: how to make it quicker + consistent. • Challenge: what “work smarter” means here. 2. (5–25) I-do → we-do — what CSS is. A rule like `h2 { color: red; }` styles EVERY `

` at once, kept in one separate file. Match the property to what it changes (drag). Model styling `` so every image gets a border. • Support: card-sort inline-style vs CSS-file. • Core: why a CSS file wins when a tag repeats. • Challenge: write a new rule. Likely error: style written in the HTML not the CSS → fix-words “the rule goes in the CSS file.” 3. (25–50) You-do — apply a CSS scheme to a page (order the set-up steps), then extend it (explorer: research more properties). Keep a screenshot. • Support: handout of the steps. • Core: style the tags listed. • Challenge: assess the benefit (“change once, update everywhere”). TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” 4. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; one reason CSS beats inline. TA notes who can explain it. Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Searching the web — crawling, indexing & ranking
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what a search engine is
I can explain how a crawler finds and indexes pages
I can describe what makes a page rank higher
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: crawler · spider · index · query · keywords · ranking. TA: the search-results and crawler images on screen; pupils continue their website. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — name the parts of a search (label-a-diagram on the starter worksheet: search box, web address, result title, description, “people also ask”). A search engine helps you FIND pages.
   • Support: tick “find web pages”.  • Core: what you type in.  • Challenge: why you only look at the first few of millions.
2. (5–25) I-do → we-do — crawlers and indexing. A crawler visits a page, reads metatags, records keywords + their frequency and the hyperlinks, stores it in the index, then follows a link. Match the word to its meaning (drag). Read an example page like a crawler.
   • Support: card-sort recorded vs ignored (small words like “the”).  • Core: list three keywords for a page.  • Challenge: analyse ranking. Likely error: thinks the engine stores the page → fix-words “it stores a RECORD of the page, not the page itself.”
3. (25–45) You-do — design a quality page that would rank well (clear headings, keywords near the top, metatags, good images, updated). Keep a screenshot; peer-review against criteria.
   • Support: the “quality web page” checklist.  • Core: build it.  • Challenge: add metatags. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
4. (45–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; one thing a crawler records. TA notes who can explain ranking.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Tightening the web — search operators & hyperlinks
lessons.objectives:
I can use search operators to control my results
I can explain how an operator changes the number of results
I can create a hyperlink between two web pages
lessons.outline: Routine first. Vocabulary on board: operator · AND · OR · NOT · quote search · hyperlink. TA: pupils have their website pages open; a search engine ready. Read the three “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–15) Starter — too many results (think–pair–share on the millions-of-results image). Use the animal-groups example: narrow vs broaden a question. • Support: tick “carefully chosen”. • Core: one way to find fewer, more useful results. • Challenge: why two people get different results. 2. (15–35) I-do → we-do — search operators. `OR` broadens (either word), `NOT`/`-` removes a word, `" "` finds the exact phrase — all change the NUMBER of results. Match the operator to its effect (drag); try the Turing/Babbage and Taj-Mahal searches. • Support: card-sort broadens vs narrows. • Core: which search gives the most results. • Challenge: write a search with an operator. 3. (35–50) You-do — make a new page summarising this lesson and HYPERLINK it to a previous page (Parsons: order the `` tag). Keep a screenshot. • Support: a hyperlink template to copy. • Core: link two pages. • Challenge: link both ways. Likely error: link not closed → fix-words “the `` tag needs a ``.” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” 4. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; did your operator broaden or narrow? TA notes who linked their pages. Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Navigating the web — navigation & end-of-unit assessment
lessons.objectives:
I can write a hyperlink with the a href tag
I can add the same navigation to every page
I can show what I have learned in the end-of-unit quiz
lessons.outline: Routine first. Vocabulary on board: navigation · hyperlink. TA: pupils’ pages from lessons 1–5 open; the quiz ready for the end. Read the three “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–5) Starter — working hyperlink or not? (spot the correct link on the starter worksheet; order a link as Parsons). Draw out: close with ``, and it is `href` not `link`. • Support: tick “move to another page”. • Core: order the hyperlink. • Challenge: say what is missing from a broken link. 2. (5–30) I-do → we-do — build the final “How to build your first web page” page summarising the unit (short, clear steps; what software; how to test). 3. (30–45) You-do — add the SAME navigation menu to every page so a visitor can move around (order the navigation steps; combine `` + `` for an image link). Keep a screenshot; test every link. • Support: a ready-made menu to paste. • Core: link all pages. • Challenge: make an image a link. Likely error: a link goes nowhere → fix-words “check the file name in `href` matches the page exactly.” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” 4. (45–55) End-of-unit quiz — the ten-question quiz worksheet, done calmly and on their own; allow plenty of time. Then self-tick the three “I can…”. Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.
